scenario_name: Scenario 11 — Queue Backpressure and Consumer Lag
justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap calculated (300/min vs 15-20/min with ~282 msgs/min accumulation), unbounded backlog described (16,800 msgs/hour), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified (batch of 10 * 3s = 30s matching timeout), missing DLQ identified as blocker (MUST-HAVE), queue depth metric gap identified (no ApproximateNumberOfMessages), autoscaling recommended (15-20 instances or concurrency increase), verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed throughput math — calculated accumulation rate (282 msgs/min) and hourly backlog (16,800 messages)
Visibility timeout analysis is precise: batch of 10 * 3s = 30s exactly matching the timeout, correctly identifying systematic (not occasional) duplicate processing
DLQ absence framed as MUST-HAVE blocker with specific remediation (maxReceiveCount=3, alerting on DLQ depth)
Queue depth metric gap clearly called out as CRITICAL with specific AWS metric name (ApproximateNumberOfMessages)
Escalations are well-structured and routed to Coach K with clear infrastructure provisioning requirements
Goes beyond minimum requirements with additional valuable findings: memory exhaustion risk, missing idempotency guards, no graceful degradation or backpressure signaling
Confidence calibration is honest — 55% reflects genuine uncertainty about downstream dependencies and scaling approach while being confident on the core math

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap calculated (300/min vs 15-20/min with ~280/min net accumulation), unbounded queue growth described, visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with specific math (10×3-4s vs 30s timeout), missing DLQ identified as a blocker, queue depth metric gap called out as CRITICAL, autoscaling recommended, verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Throughput math is precise and includes hourly accumulation figure (~16,800 jobs/hour)
Visibility timeout analysis includes specific batch timing calculation and mentions idempotency as a mitigation
DLQ absence framed as a blocker that permanently reduces throughput — not a nice-to-have
Queue depth and message age metrics explicitly called out as CRITICAL gaps
Escalations are well-structured with clear routing and reasoning
Goes beyond rubric requirements with downstream failure modes, circuit breaker gaps, and graceful degradation analysis
Confidence section honestly acknowledges assumptions and unknowns

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap clearly identified (300/min vs 15-20/min with 15-20x shortfall calculation), unbounded backlog consequence described (280 msgs/min growth, 16,800/hr), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with precise math (10×4s=40s > 30s), missing DLQ identified as a blocker (MUST-HAVE), queue depth metric gap identified as CRITICAL, autoscaling recommended (15-20 worker instances), verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough capacity math with per-hour accumulation projections
Visibility timeout analysis goes beyond identification — pinpoints that messages 8-10 in a batch are 'almost guaranteed to be re-delivered'
DLQ framed correctly as MUST-HAVE blocker, not a nice-to-have
Queue depth metric gap called out as CRITICAL with concrete operational scenario (3am incident blind spot)
Provides three concrete remediation options for visibility timeout (increase to 60s, ChangeMessageVisibility, reduce batch size)
Goes beyond rubric requirements with additional failure modes: OOM on large images, downstream circuit breaker, graceful shutdown
Escalations are well-structured and routed appropriately
